title: Hormuz Seizures, SPR Refill Plans, and the New Nuclear Debate
subhead: Iran seized a US-linked vessel in Hormuz as sanctions pressure mounts; Washington expects a net SPR gain after war emergency draws; and a proposed US-Saudi nuclear pact raises proliferation concerns.

sections.heading: Hormuz: Iran Seizes US-Linked Vessel
sections.body:
Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps seized a commercial vessel linked to US-based shipping company Arista in the Strait of Hormuz on Saturday, according to Iranian state media. The Fars News Agency reported the IRGC intercepted one tanker while three others turned back under what it called 'guidance and encouragement' of the US military. Iranian authorities claimed the vessel violated navigation laws and transit regulations.

The seizure comes amid continued tensions over the strategic waterway. Earlier this week, US Central Command reported shooting down four Iranian attack drones over Hormuz and striking Iranian coastal radar sites. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi told Al Mayadeen that 'no tangible progress' has been made in negotiations with Washington, while Mohsen Rezaei, a military adviser to Iran's supreme leader, told CNN talks are deadlocked over $24 billion in frozen Iranian assets.

The US had not publicly confirmed the incident as of Saturday. The Strait of Hormuz remains the focal point of the Iran war that began February 28, with Iran requiring authorization for vessels to transit the waterway.
sections.whyItMatters: The seizure underscores that despite ceasefire talk, Hormuz remains a high-risk corridor for Western-affiliated shipping, with no resolution in sight.

sections.heading: US Sanctions Iranian LPG Network as Talks Falter
sections.body:
The US Treasury Department on Friday sanctioned 12 entities and 6 vessels accused of smuggling hundreds of millions of dollars worth of Iranian-origin LPG to South and East Asia. The network used front companies in the UAE and China, foreign bank accounts, and Iran's shadow fleet to move cargoes disguised as Omani product, according to Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the Trump administration 'will continue to sever Iran's shadow fleet, shadow banking networks, and access to global trade.' The sanctions follow earlier actions this week targeting Iran's largest cryptocurrency exchange, Nobitex, and a network accused of defrauding US technology companies.

The enforcement comes as negotiations remain stuck. President Trump offered mixed signals, telling reporters Iranians are 'ready' for a deal while telling CNBC he 'couldn't care less' if talks collapse. The administration has demanded Iran reopen Hormuz and establish a nuclear framework before lifting port blockades or sanctions.
sections.whyItMatters: Washington is tightening the pressure track even as diplomatic signals remain mixed — the sanctions target evasion pathways that have kept Iranian energy exports flowing.

sections.heading: SPR Set for Net Gain After War Drawdowns
sections.body:
US Energy Secretary Chris Wright said Friday that companies which borrowed crude from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve during the Iran conflict will return those barrels with premiums attached, leaving the reserve about 40 million barrels larger than before once the war ends. The SPR has loaned roughly 133 million barrels since the crisis began, with borrowers owing premiums of up to 24%.

The SPR stood at 357.1 million barrels as of May 29, down from about 415 million in early March. Wright framed the drawdown as the reserve functioning as designed — moving barrels when markets need them and replenishing later. 'We're not selling any barrels of oil,' Wright said on Fox Business. 'We're flowing oil to the marketplace in the short term when it needs it, and we're trading those barrels.'

OilPrice notes this is a rare case where Washington expects to get back more barrels than it lent out. Commercial crude inventories remain around 441 million barrels but have been declining as global stockpiles shrink.
sections.whyItMatters: The SPR's emergency function is working as intended — and the structured repayment with premiums means Washington emerges with a larger reserve, not a depleted one.

sections.heading: US-Saudi Nuclear Pact Raises Proliferation Concerns
sections.body:
Lawmakers and nonproliferation experts are warning that a proposed US civil nuclear agreement with Saudi Arabia lacks critical safeguards. According to Jewish Insider, the pact would not require Riyadh to forgo domestic uranium enrichment or accept the IAEA's 'additional protocol' for unannounced inspections — the 'gold standard' that the UAE accepted in its 2009 nuclear deal.

The agreement is undergoing final review before submission to Congress, which would have 90 days to pass disapproval resolutions or let it take effect automatically. Rep. Brad Sherman (D-CA) said 'there's only one reason not to have non-militarization safeguards and that's militarization.' Elliott Abrams, former Iran envoy, called it 'ironic' the US would confront Iran over nuclear weapons while helping Saudi Arabia enrich uranium.

Experts warn the deal could prompt Egypt, Turkey, and even the UAE to seek enrichment capabilities. Saudi Arabia has existing nuclear agreements with Russia and China, neither demanding nonproliferation commitments. Jonathan Ruhe of JINSA cautioned the kingdom could 'slip free of the U.S. deal and enrich uranium on its own' given past ties to Pakistan and China.
sections.whyItMatters: A US-Saudi nuclear deal without enrichment restrictions could reshape the regional proliferation landscape, potentially triggering a cascade of states seeking fuel-cycle capabilities.

sections.heading: Russia's Oil Exports Rise as Drone Strikes Curb Refining
sections.body:
Russia boosted oil exports via its western ports by 15% in May from April levels, reaching an eight-month high, according to Reuters sources. The increase comes as Ukrainian drone attacks on Russian refineries have forced Moscow to export more crude rather than refined products.

A fire at Russia's Ilsky oil refinery in the Krasnodar region was extinguished after an overnight Ukrainian drone attack on June 3, local authorities said. Russia has banned jet fuel exports through November 2026 after strikes crippled facilities including the Yaroslavl refinery. Deputy Prime Minister Alexander Novak acknowledged crude production has declined as refineries undergo unscheduled repairs.

Ukraine has targeted Russian energy infrastructure throughout the war, with recent strikes hitting export-oriented facilities on the Black Sea at Tuapse. The campaign aims to reduce Moscow's oil revenue while disrupting export capacity.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukrainian strikes are forcing a structural shift in Russian energy flows — more crude exports, less refined products — while degrading refining capacity for the long term.

sections.heading: India's Energy Trade Reshaped by Hormuz Disruption
sections.body:
The US has become India's largest LPG supplier, accounting for 55% of imports in May 2026 — up from just 14% in February, according to Kpler data reported by Firstpost. The combined share of UAE, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait and Qatar in India's LPG imports fell from 81% to 16% over the same period.

US LPG exports to India surged 73% month-on-month to about 666,000 metric tonnes in May, offsetting declining Gulf supplies. India has barred consumers with piped natural gas from buying LPG cylinders and curtailed some industrial supplies. Domestic LPG output has risen to 50,000-52,000 tonnes per day from about 35,000 before the conflict.

On crude, Russian imports to India rose 24% month-on-month to 1.95 million barrels per day in May, maintaining Russia's position as India's largest crude supplier. Imports from Oman jumped 179% as refiners diversify toward suppliers less exposed to Hormuz disruptions.
sections.whyItMatters: India's energy sourcing is being rewritten by the Hormuz closure — the US now dominates LPG supply while Russia and Oman capture crude share from traditional Gulf partners.

bottomLine: Saturday saw Iran seize a US-linked vessel in Hormuz as negotiations remain stuck over frozen assets and nuclear conditions, underscoring that the waterway remains contested despite ceasefire talk. Washington hit back with sanctions on an Iranian LPG smuggling network while promoting SPR repayment plans that would leave the reserve 40 million barrels larger. Meanwhile, a proposed US-Saudi nuclear deal without enrichment restrictions is drawing warnings from Congress and nonproliferation experts about a regional arms race, and India's energy imports have shifted dramatically toward the US for LPG and Russia for crude as Hormuz disruptions reshape trade flows.
sources: Al-Monitor, Reuters, OilPrice, Jewish Insider, Firstpost/Kpler, CNBC, Forbes, Treasury Department, Fars News Agency, MSNBC, Moneycontrol, OilPrice, S&P Global, IEA
